Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd try to set aside time on weekends since I work during the week, but something always came up. I learn best when I can actually do things with my hands, like practice problems or work through examples physically, so I'd print stuff out when I could get to a library. Most weeks I'd cram in about 20 hours somehow, but it never felt organized or planned out properly.
Q2: My pattern was super inconsistent. Some weeks I'd be on top of things, other weeks I'd barely look at course materials until a deadline was breathing down my neck. Working and not having reliable internet at home meant I had to grab study time whenever I could access a computer somewhere. It was stressful never knowing if I'd have enough time to actually understand the material.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being real. I'd log in when I remembered an assignment was due or when I got anxious about falling behind. Sometimes I'd get a notification about something and that would push me to check in. The gamified tasks actually helped because earning points or badges gave me a small reason to log in even when I wasn't feeling it.
Q4: Not really, no. I wanted it to become routine but without internet at home, I couldn't just casually check in. I had to make special trips to campus or the library, which made everything feel like a big production. The closest I got to routine was trying to log in every time I was already on campus for something else.
Q5: The video lectures were actually helpful when I could watch them because I could pause and rewind when I got confused. I watched over 200 minutes of them, which surprised me honestly. The gamified tasks felt more engaging than regular assignments because at least there was some sense of progress or achievement, even if small. Discussion posts were okay when people actually responded to each other.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary resources. They felt overwhelming and I didn't have time to go through everything. Some of the discussion prompts felt pointless, like we were just posting to post. I'd skip anything that seemed optional because I was already struggling to keep up with required stuff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to the exam or assignments. If it was hands-on or interactive, I was more likely to engage with it. The gamified elements helped because at least I felt like I was accomplishing something even when I was scared about the course overall. Anything that felt like busy work got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist for me. I was so overwhelmed with required work and life stuff that optional felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required activities at least had consequences attached, so I'd force myself through them even when my anxiety was through the roof. Optional stuff just added to my stress because it felt like more things I wasn't doing.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download PDFs and videos whenever I had internet access, then study them at home or at work during breaks. I used a lot of handwritten notes because that's how I actually learn - I need to physically write things out. I also watched YouTube videos explaining concepts because sometimes outside explanations clicked better for me.
Q10: Most of my actual studying happened offline with printed materials and my notebook. I'd work through practice problems by hand for hours, but that never showed up anywhere. Sometimes I'd study with a coworker who was also in school, and we'd quiz each other on breaks. The LMS would just show I downloaded something once, not that I spent days working through it.
Q11: All the time, that was basically my whole strategy. I'd have one good session where I'd download everything I could, then I'd be offline studying for days. My assignment scores were decent because I actually learned the material, just not in ways the system could track. It was frustrating because I felt like my effort was invisible.
Q12: There were weeks when my anxiety got so bad I could barely function. I'd feel scared about opening the LMS because I knew I'd see all the things I was behind on. When work got busy or I had family stuff going on, the course just had to wait. My stress levels were constantly high and sometimes I'd just shut down completely.
Q13: Everything affected how I used the LMS. Being tired after work meant I'd stare at videos without absorbing anything. Confusion about assignments made me avoid them until the last minute. Not having internet at home was the biggest barrier - I couldn't just quickly check something or ask a question when I needed to. It all piled up into this overwhelming mess.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd log in and just scroll through stuff without really engaging. My brain would be too fried to actually learn anything, but I felt like I should at least be looking at course materials. I'd watch videos while thinking about a hundred other things. It was like going through the motions without actually being present.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even though it made my anxiety worse. The fear of failing or doing poorly made me avoid starting things. It sounds backwards but the more important something was, the scarier it felt to attempt it. I'd tell myself I'd do it when I felt more ready, but that readiness never really came.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have these panic sessions right before things were due where I'd finally force myself to finish. My assignment completion rate was pretty low because sometimes even deadlines weren't enough to push through the fear. The gamified tasks with smaller deadlines were actually easier to handle than big assignments.
Q17: When I got decent feedback, it helped me feel less scared about the next assignment. But honestly, I often avoided looking at feedback because I was afraid of what it would say. The times I did engage with feedback, it helped me understand what I was doing wrong. I wish I'd been better about actually using it.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming to me. There was so much content and I never felt like I could catch up once I fell behind. The gamified elements were a bright spot because they broke things into smaller, less scary pieces. But overall, the structure assumed students had more time and consistent access than I did.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they felt achievable. What made it harder was the amount of content and the assumption that everyone could access things regularly. Having more flexibility with deadlines would have helped a lot. The discussion requirements felt forced and added stress without adding much learning value for me.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me - logging in, looking around, feeling overwhelmed, and then not being able to actually complete things. High anxiety can make you check things obsessively without being able to act on them. Or maybe they're trying to learn but struggling to translate that into submitted work. The gap between understanding and producing is real.
Q21: That's kind of me, actually. My exam score wasn't great but I passed, and a lot of my learning happened offline where nobody could see it. If you just looked at my LMS activity, you'd think I wasn't trying, but I was studying in ways the system couldn't track. Some people just learn differently and don't need to be constantly online to understand material.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything. They miss all the offline studying, the mental energy spent worrying about the course, the real learning that happens away from screens. They probably make students like me look worse than we actually are because our engagement is invisible.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in effort, even when it's hard. For me, it's not about how many times I log in - it's about whether I'm genuinely trying to understand concepts. I was engaged with this course even though my anxiety made it hard to show that engagement in traditional ways. It's about the mental investment, not just the clicks.
Q24: The LMS can't show how scared I was the entire semester, or how hard I worked offline with limited resources. It can't show that I actually cared about passing and spent hours studying in ways that left no digital trace. My resilience in pushing through despite high anxiety and stress - that's invisible. The data makes me look disengaged when really I was fighting hard just to stay afloat.
